nodes.title: Return non-objects as-is

anchor.snippet: if (value === null || typeof value !== 'object') return value;

nodes.why: Primitives and null do not need deep cloning. Returning early keeps the function fast.
nodes.alternative: Try to process every value through object cloning logic.
nodes.tradeoff: Early exit is simple and efficient, but relies on correct type checks.

nodes.title: Handle Date and RegExp explicitly

anchor.snippet: if (value instanceof Date) return new Date(value.getTime());

nodes.why: These types carry internal state that plain object copying would not preserve correctly.
nodes.alternative: Skip special handling and treat them like plain objects.
nodes.tradeoff: More branches, but much more correct behavior for common special objects.

nodes.title: Recursively clone nested arrays and objects
nodes.why: Recursion ensures nested references are copied deeply, not shared.
nodes.alternative: Use shallow copy helpers like spread or Object.assign.
nodes.tradeoff: Deep copy correctness is better, but recursion can be costly on very large inputs.

nodes.title: Short-circuit already seen objects
nodes.why: This prevents infinite recursion when data contains circular references.
nodes.alternative: Assume input is acyclic and skip tracking.
nodes.tradeoff: Safer for real-world graphs, but adds WeakMap overhead.

nodes.title: Register clone before cloning children
nodes.why: Saving the container early lets recursive calls resolve cycles back to the same clone.
nodes.alternative: Set in WeakMap after child recursion finishes.
nodes.tradeoff: Early registration is required for cycles, though it can feel less intuitive initially.

nodes.title: Pass shared WeakMap through recursion
nodes.why: All nested calls must share the same map to correctly detect repeated references.
nodes.alternative: Create a new WeakMap in each recursive call.
nodes.tradeoff: Shared state gives correct graph cloning; per-call maps would break cycle handling.
